behaviour.mcq: Select one option. Answer revealed immediately on selection.

questions.stem: Epidemiology is best defined as the study of:

options.B: The distribution and determinants of disease frequency in populations, applied to disease control

questions.explanation: Epidemiology studies distribution & determinants of disease frequency in populations and applies this to control health problems.

questions.stem: Who is regarded as the father of demography, having created life tables from London death records?
questions.explanation: John Graunt — analysed parish death records and built the first life tables.

questions.stem: Which of the following is a measure of disease FREQUENCY (not association)?
questions.explanation: Prevalence is a frequency measure; the others are measures of association.

questions.stem: Prevalence depends on incidence and:
questions.explanation: Prevalence ≈ incidence × duration.

questions.stem: A dynamic (open) population is characterized by:

options.A: Permanent membership defined by an event
options.B: Transient membership defined by being in a state/condition

questions.explanation: A dynamic/open population has transient membership (e.g. residents of a city).

questions.stem: In a town of 50,000 residents, 1,500 people currently have type 2 diabetes on 1 July. What is the point prevalence of diabetes?
questions.explanation: Prevalence = existing cases ÷ total population. Worked solution: Prevalence = 1,500 / 50,000 = 0.03 = 3%

questions.stem: Which study design is most efficient for studying a RARE disease?
questions.explanation: Case-control starts from cases → efficient for rare diseases.

questions.stem: In a cohort study, subjects are grouped by their:

options.A: Disease status, then exposure is traced backward
options.B: Exposure status, then followed forward for the outcome

questions.explanation: Cohorts sample on exposure and follow forward to the outcome.

questions.stem: The measure of association typically derived from a cohort study is:
questions.explanation: Cohorts yield relative risk (risk or rate ratio).

questions.stem: An ambidirectional (bidirectional) study contains:
questions.explanation: Ambidirectional cohorts have both prospective and retrospective components.

questions.stem: 1,200 people are screened; 200 already have the disease and are excluded. The remaining are followed for 4 years, during which 150 develop the disease (no losses). What is the 4-year cumulative incidence?
questions.explanation: Cumulative incidence = new cases ÷ population AT RISK at start (exclude prevalent cases). Worked solution: Population at risk = 1,200 − 200 = 1,000 CI = 150 / 1,000 = 0.15 = 15% over 4 years

questions.stem: The three components of the epidemiologic triangle are:
questions.explanation: Host · Agent (pathogen) · Environment.

questions.stem: John Snow is credited with demonstrating that:

options.A: Scurvy is caused by vitamin deficiency
options.B: Cholera is transmitted by faecally-contaminated water
options.C: Smallpox can be prevented by vaccination

questions.explanation: Snow linked cholera to contaminated drinking water (Broad St pump).

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT a Bradford Hill criterion for causation?
questions.explanation: Randomization is a design method, not a Hill criterion.

questions.stem: The Bradford Hill criterion of 'temporality' means that:

options.B: The cause must precede the effect
options.C: The dose relates to the response

questions.explanation: Temporality: the exposure (cause) must come before the outcome.

questions.stem: A study accumulates 2,500 person-years of follow-up, during which 50 new cases of disease occur. What is the incidence rate?
questions.explanation: Incidence rate = new cases ÷ total person-time. Worked solution: IR = 50 / 2,500 = 0.02 per person-year = 20 per 1,000 person-years

questions.stem: A confounder must satisfy all of the following EXCEPT:

options.A: It is associated with the exposure
options.B: It is an independent risk factor for the disease
options.C: It lies on the causal pathway between exposure and disease
options.D: It is not an effect of the exposure

questions.explanation: A confounder must NOT be an intermediate on the causal pathway.

questions.stem: Randomization in a trial is most valuable because it:

options.B: Balances both known AND unknown confounders (with large n)
options.D: Removes the need for a control group

questions.explanation: Randomization balances confounders you can't even measure.

questions.stem: The main bacterial indicator organism used to assess water quality is:
questions.explanation: E. coli (faecal coliform) is the principal indicator of faecal contamination.

questions.stem: At a banquet, 250 people ate the buffet and 75 developed gastroenteritis within 12 hours. What is the attack rate?
questions.explanation: Attack rate = new cases ÷ population at risk during the outbreak. Worked solution: Attack rate = 75 / 250 = 0.30 = 30%

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT a method of disinfecting drinking water?
questions.explanation: Aluminium sulfate is a coagulant (clarifies water) — not a disinfectant.

questions.stem: Cryptosporidium, responsible for the 1993 Milwaukee outbreak, is a:
questions.explanation: Cryptosporidium is a waterborne protozoan parasite (a classic trick — not a bacterium).

questions.stem: Ground-level ozone (O₃) is classified as a:
questions.explanation: O₃ forms by atmospheric reactions → secondary pollutant.

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT a component of clean, unpolluted air?
questions.explanation: Ammonia is not a component of clean air.

questions.stem: In a cohort, 90 of 300 smokers develop disease and 30 of 300 non-smokers develop disease. What is the risk ratio for smokers?
questions.explanation: Risk ratio = risk in exposed ÷ risk in unexposed. Worked solution: Risk (smokers) = 90/300 = 0.30 Risk (non-smokers) = 30/300 = 0.10 RR = 0.30 / 0.10 = 3.0

questions.stem: The primary anthropogenic greenhouse gas is:
questions.explanation: CO₂ is the dominant greenhouse gas.

questions.stem: Foodborne INTOXICATION (as opposed to infection) is characterized by:

options.A: Multiplication of the microbe in the intestine
options.B: A pre-formed toxin present in the food

questions.explanation: Intoxication is caused by a pre-formed toxin in the food (no intestinal multiplication needed).

questions.stem: Aflatoxins, the most widespread mycotoxins, are produced by Aspergillus and found mainly in:
questions.explanation: Aflatoxins contaminate cereal grains & nuts.

questions.stem: According to WHO, the safe occupational noise limit for an 8-hour shift is approximately:
questions.explanation: WHO safe threshold ≈ < 75 dB for an 8-h shift.

questions.stem: The Nuremberg Code is best known for:

options.B: Ten principles emphasizing voluntary informed consent

questions.explanation: The Nuremberg Code = 10 principles stressing voluntary informed consent.
